query: evidence on cost-effectiveness of fecal immunochemical tests versus traditional colonoscopies

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fecal Immune-Chemical Test: This test employs antibodies to specifically detect human heme-based globin. Dietary and medication restrictions prior to the test aren't required. The test is very specific for detecting colonic/rectal bleeding. Advantages Bowel preparation isn't a prerequisite. Dietary or medication restrictions are not a prerequisite for FIT. Samples can be collected at home, hence convenience and higher adherence. [34] Cost-effective compared to other CRC screening tests. There is no risk of damage to the colon. Disadvantages The test does not detect some polyps and cancers. False-positive test results are possible. Dietary restrictions are needed before guaiac FOBT. Additional procedures, such as colonoscopy, may be needed if the results are positive.

retrieved_docs.title: Health Screening -- Clinical Significance -- Colorectal Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Both the incidence and the mortality rates from colorectal cancer have been decreasing in the United States for a number of years, partly (although not exclusively) because of increased screening. There are two general groups of screening tests available. Stool-based tests include the guaiac-based fecal occult blood test (gFOBT), the fecal immunochemical test (FIT), and the FIT-DNA test. Endoscopic and radiologic tests include flexible sigmoidoscopy, colonoscopy, and CT colonography (CTC). These tests vary as to cost, ease, sensitivity, and specificity. None of these tests have been found to be superior in head-to-head trials, so at this time they are all considered viable options for colorectal cancer screening. Until one screening test is demonstrated to be better than the others, any screening test is better than none. Tests such as digital rectal exams and air-contrast barium enema are no longer widely recommended for colorectal cancer screening. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Fecal Occult Blood Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) concludes with high certainty that screening for colorectal cancer in adults aged 50 to 75 years has a substantial net benefit, and screening for colorectal cancer in adults aged 45 to 49 years has a moderate net benefit. [63] The USPSTF also concludes with moderate certainty that screening for colorectal cancer in adults aged 76 to 85 years who have been previously screened has a small net benefit. This assessment of net benefit applies to stool-based tests with high sensitivity, colonoscopy, computed tomography (CT) colonography, and flexible sigmoidoscopy. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Clinical Significance -- Evidence of Effectiveness of Various Screening Tests:
retrieved_docs.content: Guaiac FOBT vs F ecal Immune-chemical Test FIT is more sensitive than gFOBT for colon lesions. [15] High-sensitivity gFOBT has a sensitivity of 62% to 79% and a specificity of 87% to 96% for detecting colorectal cancer. [16] FIT has a sensitivity of 79% to 88% and a specificity of 91% to 93%. Evidence suggests a decline in the mortality rate by 15% to 33% when gFOBT/FIT is performed every 1 to 2 years in people 50 to 80 years. [16] FIT has high sensitivity (80%) for detecting CRC, while only 25% to 56% sensitivity is for detecting advanced adenomas. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: UK Screening and Surveillance For Bowel Cancers -- Other Issues -- Alternatives to Colonoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy is considered the gold standard for colorectal cancer screening and surveillance due to its ability to provide direct visualisation of the colon and rectum. However, there are alternate screening and diagnostic methods, each with advantages and limitations. Commonly employed alternatives include sigmoidoscopy, virtual colonoscopy, barium enema, and capsule endoscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Clinical Significance -- Screening Frequencies and Ideal Intervals for Surveillance and Follow-up
retrieved_docs.content: Guaiac FOBT and F ecal Immune-chemical Test Frequency of testing: Experts recommend sigmoidoscopy every 5 years for people at average risk who have had negative test results. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: UK Screening and Surveillance For Bowel Cancers -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity presents evidence-based strategies within the UK National Health Service, epidemiology, aetiology, and pathogenesis, uncovering the roles of genetic, environmental factors, and the adenoma-carcinoma sequence in disease progression. Diverse screening guidelines across UK nations, from faecal occult blood tests to faecal immunochemical tests, and their impact on patient engagement rates are discussed. Insights into detailed surveillance protocols for hereditary colorectal cancer and postsurgical management recommendations by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence are provided. Contradictions, alternatives to colonoscopy, and the pivotal role of the interprofessional team in advancing screening methods, guideline adherence, and personalized patient care are highlighted.

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Screening -- Function -- Stool-Based Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Stool deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) testing uses Cologuard—the first multitarget stool DNA test approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in August 2014 for CRC screening. Cologuard detects abnormal DNA present in stool samples from individuals with malignancies. Comparative studies between FIT and stool DNA testing indicate higher sensitivity but lower specificity and increased false-positive rates for the latter. [19] As per USPSTF guidelines, screening should commence at age 50, utilizing stool-based tests (gFOBT, FIT, and FIT-DNA) or direct visualization tests (colonoscopy every 10 years, sigmoidoscopy every 5 years, or CT colonography every 5 years, if combined with annual FIT).

retrieved_docs.title: Fecal Occult Blood Test -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The fecal occult blood test (FOBT) is a diagnostic test to assess for hidden (occult) blood in the stool. This test is commonly used for colorectal cancer screening, especially in developed nations. Colon cancer is one of the most prevalent cancers in both men and women worldwide. Therefore, early detection is imperative. When used correctly for screening, this testing modality has established associations with decreased morbidity and mortality. [1] Newer screening methods have been developed, including the fecal immunochemical test (FIT). FIT uses antibodies to discern blood in the stool. These newer modalities have replaced the FOBT for colon cancer screening due to increased specificity, sensitivity, and decreased costs. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UK Screening and Surveillance For Bowel Cancers -- Other Issues -- Contraindications to Screening and Surveillance for Colorectal Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Patients of any age with a life expectancy of less than 10 years, for whom the procedural risks or burden may not outweigh the benefits of early detection and intervention. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: UK Screening and Surveillance For Bowel Cancers -- Other Issues -- Contraindications to Screening and Surveillance for Colorectal Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Patients 75 years or older, for whom the procedural risks or burden may not outweigh the benefits of early detection and intervention. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: UK Screening and Surveillance For Bowel Cancers -- Other Issues -- Alternatives to Colonoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Sigmoidoscopy is less invasive than colonoscopy. While sigmoidoscopy may be employed for colorectal screening or surveillance, the procedure examines only the sigmoid colon and rectum; complete visualisation of the colon is not performed. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Rectal Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Utilize CRC screening guidelines to guide and avoid repeating other screening modalities after a high-quality colonoscopy for ten years or with less than ten years of life expectancy.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Clinical Significance -- Stool DNA Test
retrieved_docs.content: Also known as the FIT-DNA test, it comes as a US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved kit. This is a multi-target test that detects occult blood along with 9 DNA biomarkers of three genes associated with CRC and advanced adenoma. [8] [18] Sample collection: Like FOBTs, the patient is provided with a stool collection kit and asked to collect a stool sample, which can be delivered via mail or personally to a laboratory/office, ideally within 72 hours. Advantages No bowel preparation is required. No dietary or medication restrictions as a pre-requisite. Samples can be collected at home, hence convenience and high adherence. No risk of damage to the colon. Disadvantages More expensive than gFOBT or FIT. Test sensitivity for adenomas is low. False-positive test results can be seen. Additional procedures, such as colonoscopy, are advised if the results are positive.

retrieved_docs.title: Fecal Occult Blood Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Colorectal cancer screening is now recommended for average-risk individuals starting at age 45, according to the American College of Gastroenterology guidelines. [65] In patients with first-degree relatives with advanced adenomas or colorectal carcinoma diagnosed before age 60, screening should begin at age 40 years or ten years earlier than the youngest diagnosed relative. In patients with first-degree relatives diagnosed with advanced adenomas or colorectal cancer after age 60, screening can begin at the usual age of 50. [66] Recommendations also exist that the fecal immunochemical test (FIT) replace the older guaiac-based FOBT due to increased sensitivity and specificity. [67] FIT targets human globin, often found with lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and it has been shown to improve detection rates for colorectal cancer compared to FOBT. It also does not necessitate any dietary modifications, improving patient adherence. [68]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy and biopsy is classified as a component of إجراءات تصوير البطن والجهاز الهضمي (abdominal and gastrointestinal imaging procedures) and also as part of التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي (gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures). This dual categorization reflects that colonoscopy with biopsy involves both imaging of the abdominal and digestive tract and the use of endoscopic techniques to directly visualize the colon and obtain tissue samples for diagnostic evaluation. Recognizing colonoscopy and biopsy within these broader procedural groups helps clinicians understand its role in comprehensive gastrointestinal assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2882
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagoscopy (تنظير مريئي) is classified within the broader category of other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures, indicating that it is one of several techniques used to evaluate the gastrointestinal tract and is part of a wider set of diagnostic tools for gastrointestinal assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2754
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy and biopsy is identified as a specific type of Surgical Procedures on the Colon and Rectum, indicating that it is a surgical intervention performed on the colon and rectum. At the same time, colonoscopy and biopsy is placed under the broader category of إجراءات تصوير البطن والجهاز الهضمي (abdominal and gastrointestinal imaging procedures), showing that it also belongs to the general group of imaging techniques used to evaluate the gastrointestinal tract. This dual classification underscores that colonoscopy and biopsy serves both as a targeted surgical procedure for the colon and rectum and as an integral component of comprehensive abdominal and gastrointestinal imaging in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2733
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy and biopsy is identified as the principal diagnostic procedure for colorectal cancer, corresponding to the PhenX data element that captures the detection time point for a patient in a nominal format. This procedure is classified as a specific type of surgical intervention on the colon and rectum, falling under the broader category of Surgical Procedures on the Colon and Rectum. By serving both as a key diagnostic tool for identifying colorectal malignancies and as a recognized surgical technique, colonoscopy and biopsy plays a central role in patient evaluation and management within colorectal disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 361
retrieved_docs.content: procedures are interconnected through a hierarchical taxonomy that links specific diagnostic and therapeutic actions (such as colonoscopy, biopsy, and esophagoscopy) to broader categories of internal endoscopic technology, gastrointestinal imaging, and surgical interventions on the digestive tract. This integrated framework clarifies how these procedures relate to one another and to the underlying interventional tools used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2883
retrieved_docs.content: التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي and other bowel diagnostic procedures are both classified as subcategories of تنظير الجهاز الهضمي. This indicates that internal gastrointestinal endoscopy and additional bowel diagnostic procedures fall under the broader umbrella of gastrointestinal endoscopy, which encompasses various endoscopic techniques used to visualize, evaluate, and diagnose conditions of the gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2796
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy and biopsy is a diagnostic and therapeutic procedure that utilizes an internal endoscope (منظار داخلي) to examine the colon and obtain tissue samples. This procedure is classified under the broader category of abdominal and gastrointestinal imaging procedures (إجراءات تصوير البطن والجهاز الهضمي) and is also a specific type of internal gastrointestinal endoscopy (التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي). By employing an internal endoscope, colonoscopy and biopsy provides direct visualization of the gastrointestinal tract and enables targeted biopsy, linking the technique to the use of endoscopic equipment while fitting within the larger frameworks of abdominal imaging and endoscopic evaluation of the digestive system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1743
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical procedures on the colon and rectum are classified as a type of gastrointestinal tract surgery, representing a specific subset of abdominal surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of gastrointestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). As a subset of this wider gastrointestinal spectrum, anal and rectal conditions affect the distal portion of the digestive tract and share many pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic considerations, and therapeutic strategies with other gastrointestinal disorders. Recognizing that anal and rectal conditions have a broader relationship with gastrointestinal disorder encourages clinicians to adopt a comprehensive, system‑based approach to evaluation and management, ensuring that patient care addresses both localized anorectal pathology and its context within the entire gastrointestinal system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: (gastrointestinal endoscopy) as a parent, further situating internal endoscopic techniques within the general endoscopy domain. Overall, the knowledge network portrays colonoscopy and biopsy as a central, multi‑faceted diagnostic and surgical procedure for colorectal disease, situated within a layered taxonomy of digestive system procedures, endoscopic techniques, and imaging modalities, all of which are organized under broader concepts of gastrointestinal endoscopy and esophageal endoscopy. This integrated classification supports comprehensive patient care by linking specific diagnostic actions to their larger procedural and anatomical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: تنظيم القولون (colonoscopic examination) is performed with منظار داخلي (internal endoscope), a flexible medical instrument that allows direct visualization of the colonic mucosa, facilitates tissue sampling, and enables therapeutic interventions during endoscopic procedures. The internal endoscope therefore serves as the essential device for conducting تنظيم القولون, providing clinicians with a minimally invasive means to diagnose and treat colonic pathology. An additional entry linking منظار داخلي to the Chinese text “关于预定行程的原因:印象:时间点:救护车运输:名义型” appears to be unrelated to the medical context and likely represents a data mismatch; it does not alter the established relationship between تنظيم القولون and منظار داخلي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1725
retrieved_docs.content: Inspection (procedure) is a diagnostic category that incorporates several specific methods for visual and functional assessment of internal organs. Among its techniques are mediastinoscopy and thoracoscopy performed with ventilation of the lung, which allow direct visualization and sampling of mediastinal and pleural structures while maintaining adequate respiratory support. Another method included under inspection is the combination of paracentesis and endoscopic examination of the ileum, enabling fluid analysis together with direct endoscopic evaluation of the distal small intestine. In addition, inspection procedures serve as the method of choice for other bowel diagnostic procedures, encompassing a range of endoscopic and minimally invasive approaches used to assess gastrointestinal pathology. Together, these methods provide clinicians with essential information for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2022
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy and ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation) are frequently observed together in clinical practice. An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1660
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy and biopsy is identified as a specific Procedure on digestive system and is placed under the broader parent category إجراءات تصوير البطن والجهاز الهضمي (abdominal and gastrointestinal imaging procedures). It is recognized as the principal diagnostic method for colorectal cancer, providing detection information at a particular time point for patients as defined by the PhenX nominal data element “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX”. This integration of procedural classification and diagnostic purpose highlights the role of colonoscopy and biopsy in gastrointestinal evaluation and colorectal cancer screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2719
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy and biopsy is a specific type of procedure on the digestive system that serves as the principal diagnostic method for colorectal cancer. According to the PhenX framework, this procedure is identified as the main diagnostic process for detecting colorectal cancer at a particular time point in patients, providing essential clinical information for disease confirmation. As a subclass of general digestive system procedures, colonoscopy and biopsy plays a central role in the evaluation and management of patients suspected of having colorectal malignancies.
